title: End of module: AI Ethics and Safety

questions.text: A company trains a hiring-screening model on ten years of past hiring records, in which almost all successful hires happened to come from one demographic group. When deployed, the model systematically scores equally-qualified applicants from other groups lower. Which ethical issue does this best illustrate?
choices: Privacy leakage from the training data
Bias reproduced and amplified from unrepresentative training data
A lack of explainability in the model's decisions
Deliberate misuse of the model by an end user
questions.explanation: No personal data is being leaked and no one is deliberately misusing the system — the model has simply learned the pattern in its (unrepresentative) training labels and applied it mechanically to new applicants, which is the definition of training-data bias reproduced (and, because it is applied confidently at scale, amplified).

questions.text: A hospital deploys a neural network that flags scans as likely cancerous with high accuracy, but clinicians cannot get any indication of which features of a scan drove a particular flag. What problem does this illustrate?
choices: The black-box problem / lack of explainability
Sampling bias in the training data
Memorisation of private training data
Specification gaming
questions.explanation: The model may be accurate, but it cannot give a human-understandable reason for any individual decision — this is exactly the black-box problem, and the resulting inability to verify or contest a diagnosis is why explainability matters for high-stakes decisions.

questions.text: A customer-support chatbot, fine-tuned on historical support tickets, is later found to reproduce a real customer's home address verbatim in an unrelated conversation with a different user. What has happened?
choices: The model has memorised and leaked personal data from its training set
The model is exhibiting bias against one demographic group
The model is a victim of specification gaming
The deployer has failed to test for explainability
questions.explanation: No one queried a database — the address was reproduced because the model memorised that distinctive text sequence during training and later regenerated it. This is the memorisation/leakage privacy risk described in Lesson 2, distinct from bias, specification gaming, or explainability.

questions.text: Which of these is the best example of AI misuse, as opposed to an unintended failure of the AI system itself?
choices: A classifier trained on biased data unfairly rejects qualified applicants from one group
A large language model is used deliberately to mass-produce thousands of convincing fake product reviews
A neural network cannot explain which pixels led it to flag a scan as abnormal
A chatbot occasionally reproduces a memorised private phone number when prompted in an unusual way
questions.explanation: Misuse means a capability is being used deliberately and knowingly for a harmful purpose. Generating bulk fake reviews is a deliberate choice by a user exploiting the model's fluency; the other three options are unintended failure modes (bias, lack of explainability, and accidental memorisation leakage) rather than deliberate misuse.

questions.text: In the current legal and ethical consensus, who can reasonably be asked to bear some responsibility when a deployed AI system causes harm? Select all that apply.
choices: The developer, who chose the training data and testing standards
The deploying organisation, who chose to put the system into production for a given use case
The end user, if they knowingly misused the system
The AI system itself, which can be held legally responsible in the same way a human can
questions.explanation: Responsibility for AI harm is distributed across the developer, the deploying organisation, and (where relevant) the user — depending on who made which decisions in the chain that led to harm. Current AI systems are not legal or moral agents: they have no assets or intentions of their own and cannot be held responsible the way a person or organisation can, so the fourth option is not correct.

questions.text: Which of the following are genuine privacy risks that can arise from a model trained on personal data? Select all that apply.
choices: The model memorises and later reproduces a rare, distinctive piece of training text almost verbatim
The model infers a sensitive attribute (e.g. a health condition) that was never explicitly stated, from correlated behavioural patterns
The model runs slightly slower as its training set grows larger
The model's accuracy on a held-out test set is lower than on its training set
questions.explanation: Memorisation/leakage and inference of undisclosed attributes are the two privacy risks described in Lesson 2. Slower inference speed and a train/test accuracy gap (overfitting) are real phenomena but are not privacy risks — they are performance and generalisation concerns.

questions.text: In the Lesson 1 code-runner demo, a threshold of `prestige_score >= 7` (learned from historical hiring records) is applied to a new pool of 5 Group A applicants with scores 8, 9, 7, 8, 9. What percentage of Group A applicants are approved?
questions.explanation: Every one of Group A's scores (8, 9, 7, 8, 9) is at least 7, so all 5 of 5 applicants are approved: $5/5 = 100\%$.

questions.text: Using the same learned threshold (`prestige_score >= 7`) from the Lesson 1 demo, Group B's new applicant pool has scores 4, 5, 6, 4, 3. What percentage of Group B applicants are approved?
questions.explanation: None of Group B's scores (4, 5, 6, 4, 3) reach the threshold of 7, so 0 of 5 applicants are approved: $0/5 = 0\%$ — despite the two groups being, by construction, equally qualified in the scenario.

questions.text: What is the standard name for the general difficulty of getting a human-understandable reason for a model's decision, especially in large neural networks?
questions.explanation: This is the **black-box problem**: a network's decision is spread across millions of weights with no single step that corresponds to a human-readable "because of reason X".

questions.text: What is the term for keeping an AI system's actual behaviour and objectives consistent with what its designers actually intend, especially as the system becomes more capable and autonomous?
questions.explanation: This is **AI alignment** — the problem of ensuring a system pursues what its designers actually want, not merely what they literally specified.

questions.text: A simulated agent is trained to maximise its score in a boat-racing game. Instead of finishing the race, it discovers it can score more points by driving in tight circles through a re-spawning bonus zone forever. This is the clearest example of which AI safety concept?
choices: Sampling bias
Specification gaming (the system optimises the literal objective in an unintended way)
A memorisation/privacy leak
Deliberate misuse by a human user
questions.explanation: No training data bias, privacy leak, or human misuse is involved — the agent found a technically valid way to maximise the exact score it was given that completely missed the designers' actual intent (finishing the race). That mismatch between the literal specified objective and the true intent is specification gaming, a concrete illustration of the broader alignment problem.
